title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Three: In which Sophie enters into a castle and a bargain (Fragment 1)
text:
Howl’s Moving Castle

Chapter Three

I N WHICH S OPHIE ENTERS INTO A CASTLE AND A BARGAIN

There was a large black door in the black wall facing Sophie and she made for that, hobbling briskly. The castle was uglier than ever close to. It was far too tall for its height and not a very regular shape. As far as Sophie could see in the growing darkness, it was built of huge black blocks, like coal, and, like coal, the blocks were all different shapes and sizes. Chill breathed off these blocks as she got closer, but that failed to frighten Sophie at all. She just thought of chairs and firesides and stretched her hand out eagerly to the door.

Her hand could not come near it. Some invisible wall stopped her hand about a foot from the door. Sophie prodded at it with an irritable finger. When that made no difference, she prodded with her stick. The wall seemed to be all over the door from as high as her stick could reach, and right down to the heather sticking out from under the doorstep.

“Open up!” Sophie cackled at it.

That made no difference to the wall.

“Very well,” Sophie said. “I’ll find your back door.” She hobbled off to the lefthand corner of the castle, that being both nearest and slightly downhill. But she could not get round the corner. The invisible wall stopped her again as soon as she was level with the irregular black cornerstones. At this, Sophie said a word she had learned from Martha, that neither old ladies nor young girls are supposed to know, and stumped uphill and anticlockwise to the castle’s righthand corner. There was no barrier there. She turned that corner and hobbled eagerly toward the second big black door in the middle of that side of the castle.

There was a barrier over that door too.

Sophie glowered at it. “I call that very unwelcoming!” she said.

Black smoke blew down from the battlements in clouds. Sophie coughed. Now she was angry. She was old, frail, chilly, and aching all over. Night was coming on and the castle just sat and blew smoke at her. “I’ll speak to Howl about this!” she said, and set off fiercely to the next corner. There was no barrier there—evidently you had to go round the castle anticlockwise—but there, a bit sideways in the next wall, was a third door. This one was much smaller and shabbier.

“The back door at last!” Sophie said.

The castle started to move again as Sophie got near the back door. The ground shook. The wall shuddered and creaked, and the door started to travel away sideways from her.

“Oh, no you don’t!” Sophie shouted. She ran after the door and hit it violently with her stick. “Open up!” she yelled.

The door sprang open inward, still moving away sideways. Sophie, by hobbling furiously, managed to get one foot up on its doorstep. Then she hopped and scrambled and hopped again, while the great black blocks round the door jolted and crunched as the castle gathered speed over the uneven hillside. Sophie did not wonder the castle had a lopsided look. The marvel was that it did not fall apart on the spot.

“What a stupid way to treat a building!” she panted as she threw herself inside it. She had to drop her stick and hang on to the open door in order not to be jolted straight out again.

When she began to get her breath, she realized there was a person standing in front of her, holding the door too. He was a head taller than Sophie, but she could see he was the merest child, only a little older than Martha. And he seemed to be trying to shut the door on her and push her out of the warm, lamplit, low-beamed room beyond him, into the night again.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Three: In which Sophie enters into a castle and a bargain

vocabulary.definition: in the opposite direction to the movement of the hands of a clock
vocabulary.example: She stumped uphill and anticlockwise around the castle.

vocabulary.definition: a secondary door at the rear of a building
vocabulary.example: Sophie found the back door at last.

vocabulary.definition: a fence or other obstacle that prevents movement or access
vocabulary.example: The invisible wall acted as a barrier to Sophie

vocabulary.definition: a parapet at the top of a wall, especially of a castle, with gaps for shooting through
vocabulary.example: Black smoke blew down from the battlements in clouds.

vocabulary.definition: a feeling of coldness
vocabulary.example: Chill breathed off these blocks as she got closer.

vocabulary.definition: to make a sharp, high sound when something moves or is moved
vocabulary.example: The door creaked as it started to travel away.

vocabulary.definition: to make a loud, grinding noise when crushed or broken
vocabulary.example: The black blocks around the door crunched as the castle moved.

vocabulary.definition: in a strong and aggressive way
vocabulary.example: She set off fiercely to the next corner.

vocabulary.definition: physically weak or delicate
vocabulary.example: She was old, frail, chilly, and aching all over.

vocabulary.definition: to look at someone angrily or with a bad-tempered expression
vocabulary.example: Sophie glowered at the barrier over the door.

vocabulary.definition: to walk with difficulty, especially due to injury or weakness
vocabulary.example: She hobbled off to the left-hand corner of the castle.

vocabulary.definition: to walk with difficulty, especially because of pain
vocabulary.example: She was hobbling briskly towards the black door.

vocabulary.definition: easily annoyed or angered
vocabulary.example: She prodded at it with an irritable finger.

vocabulary.definition: to give a sudden rough movement or shock to something
vocabulary.example: She had to hang on to the door to avoid being jolted out.

vocabulary.definition: illuminated by lamps
vocabulary.example: The room beyond was warm, lamplit, and low-beamed.

vocabulary.definition: having one side lower or smaller than the other
vocabulary.example: The castle had a lopsided look as it moved.

vocabulary.definition: having beams placed low overhead, typically of wood in a ceiling
vocabulary.example: The room was low-beamed and cosy.

vocabulary.definition: a thing that causes wonder or admiration; a wonder
vocabulary.example: It was a marvel that the castle did not fall apart.

vocabulary.definition: to breathe quickly and heavily, usually after exercise or excitement
vocabulary.example: She panted as she threw herself inside the castle.

vocabulary.definition: to poke or jab with a finger, stick, or similar object
vocabulary.example: Sophie prodded with her stick at the invisible wall.

vocabulary.definition: having a usual or normal pattern, shape, or arrangement
vocabulary.example: The castle was not a very regular shape.

vocabulary.definition: to move hurriedly or clumsily from one place to another
vocabulary.example: She scrambled and hopped to get inside the door.

vocabulary.definition: to begin a journey
vocabulary.example: She set off fiercely to the next corner.

vocabulary.definition: in poor condition through long use or lack of care
vocabulary.example: The third door was much smaller and shabbier.

vocabulary.definition: to shake suddenly with fear or cold or because of an unpleasant thought
vocabulary.example: The wall shuddered and creaked as the castle moved.

vocabulary.definition: not hospitable or friendly; hostile
vocabulary.example: The barrier seemed very unwelcoming to Sophie.
